---
title: "Introduction to Shiny Modules"
---

## Apps grow into tangled graphs

![](/img/modules-before.png){height="500px" fig-align="center"}

::: {.footnote}
Source: [Mastering Shiny, ch. 19](https://mastering-shiny.org/scaling-modules.html)
:::

## Group the pieces that belong together

![](/img/modules-after.png){height="500px" fig-align="center"}

## What changed

- Related boxes get grouped into a named unit (`session`, `data`, `results`, ...)
- The **same shape** (yellow) shows up twice — written once, used twice
- That's the whole pitch for modules: **isolate** pieces so you can reason
  about them alone, **reuse** pieces so you don't rewrite them

## A module is just a function

Abstraction is why we write functions at all — hide the details, expose a
small interface, reuse the logic without re-deriving it.

A plain function already gives you that. So why isn't it enough here?

## The same problem, at our scale

Your tips dashboard's sidebar has a slider, a checkbox group, and a
`@reactive.calc` that filters the data.

Now imagine a second dataset, with its own filters. Copy-paste the sidebar
code. Copy-paste the filter logic. Now you have **two places** to fix every
bug and **two places** to add every new feature.

## Functions don't fully solve this

Remember: every input/output **ID must be unique across the whole app**.

Python's function scoping gives two calls independent local variables for
free — but `"filter_total_bill"` isn't a variable. It's a string shipped to
the browser and tracked in Shiny's session-wide input list, and Python's
scope has no jurisdiction over it. Call the function twice and you get two
`input_slider()`s both named `"filter_total_bill"` — Shiny can't tell them
apart.

## Shiny modules

A module is a function that:

- Encapsulates UI *and* server logic together
- Gets its own **namespace** — an ID prefix Shiny manages for you, so
  duplicate inner IDs never collide
- Otherwise behaves like a normal Python function: takes arguments,
  returns values

<https://shiny.posit.co/py/docs/modules.html>

## Shiny Core: `@module.ui` / `@module.server`

```{.python}
from shiny import module, reactive, render, ui

@module.ui
def filter_ui():
    return ui.output_ui("filters")

@module.server
def filter_server(input, output, session, data, columns):
    ...
    return {"mask": mask}
```

Use it with an explicit id: `filter_ui("tips_filter")`,
`filter_server("tips_filter", data=tips, columns=[...])`.

## Shiny Express: `@module`

One function is both UI and server. Modules are supported in Express as of
Shiny 0.10.0.

```{.python}
from shiny.express import module, render, ui

@module
def filter_panel(input, output, session, data, columns):
    ...
    return {"mask": mask}
```

Use it the same way: `filter_panel("tips_filter", data=tips, columns=[...])`.

::: {.callout-important}
A module written in Core syntax can be used from an Express app, and vice
versa — the two are interchangeable.
:::

## Our filter module

```{.python}
{{< include /../code/demos/filter_module_express.py >}}
```

Numeric columns get a range slider, everything else gets a checkbox group —
same idea as the sidebar you already built by hand for tips.

## Next

We'll refactor the tips dashboard's sidebar into this module, then reuse it
on a second dataset without writing any new filter code.
